citations.narrative:
During handling of non human primates, human error resulted in the physical harm and trauma to 2 individuals. In one
case, on 3 February 2026, an ~14 year old male macaque was injured when a sliding door between two enclosures was
inadvertently opened by the caretaker. The two male individuals on either side of this door were not compatible, and in the
fight the 14 year old male macaque sustained injuries. Veterinary care was provided to treat several lacerations on the
face and shoulders, which included suturing layers to close the wounds. In the other incident, on 18 September 2025, a
clip was left behind in the outdoor enclosure after routine physical exams and a 4 year old female macaque caught the
clip on the commissure and cheek pouch of the mouth resulting in injury. Veterinary care was provided, and the cheek
area was surgically repaired, but that area is very mobile and the sutures had to be replaced on 22 September 2025. Both
individuals healed from their injuries and were discharged from hospital care and back to their home enclosures. Handling
of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma or physical
harm.
Corrected at the time of inspection on 4 March 2026

On 18 January 2026 a female macaque escaped her enclosure due to a cage weld failure on the door and engaged in an
altercation with a 13 year old female macaque still in her enclosure. The 13 year old female suffered multiple wounds,
including to the face and hands with amputations necessary to the left hand digits 2 & 5 and was provided immediate
veterinary care. The 13 year old macaque was monitored in the hospital until her wounds healed and then discharged to
her normal housing. Primary enclosures for nonhuman primates must be structurally sound for the species of nonhuman
primates contained in them and kept in good repair. The cage weld that failed was no longer in good repair and allowed
the animal to escape her enclosure and engage with the 13 year old female macaque that was injured.
Corrected at the time of inspection on 4 March 2026

In three nonhuman primate enclosures the animals were not housed in the space required by the regulation when housed
together. A male and female macaque, both ~5 years old were housed together in a pair of enclosures that only provided
4.3 square feet of space per animal. The male macaque weighed 10.3 kilograms (kg) according to the room log and
required at least 6 feet of space, for a total of 10.3 square feet of space. In another pair of 4.3 square foot cages, an 11
year old female was in one and a 9 year old female with a 7 month old infant (1.18kgs) was in the other enclosure. While
the 8.6 square feet of space was sufficient for the adult females (both less than 10 kg), the 7 month old needed an
additional 1.6 square feet of space per the regulations. In another pair of 4.3 square foot cages an ~8.5 year old female
macaque (11.9 kgs) was housed with ~1.5 year old daughter (3.15 kgs), so total required square feet was 10.3 square
feet and only 8.6 square feet was provided. Minimum space regulations are required by the Animal Welfare Act in order to
ensure the welfare of the animals contained within.
Corrected during inspection on 6 March 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the Attending Veterinarian and the IACUC staff.
Additional Inspectors:
Darren Rausch, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.38(f)(1) 
   
Miscellaneous. 
During handling of non human primates, human error resulted in the physical harm and trauma to 2 individuals. In one 
case, on 3 February 2026, an ~14 year old male macaque was injured when a sliding door between two enclosures was

area was surgically repaired, but that area is very mobile and the sutures had to be replaced on 22 September 2025. Both 
individuals healed from their injuries and were discharged from hospital care and back to their home enclosures. Handling

harm. 
 
Corrected at the time of inspection on 4 March 2026 
3.80(a)(1)  Critical
   
Primary enclosures. 
On 18 January 2026 a female macaque escaped her enclosure due to a cage weld failure on the door and engaged in an 
altercation with a 13 year old female macaque still in her enclosure. The 13 year old female suffered multiple wounds, 
including to the face and hands with amputations necessary to the left hand digits 2 & 5 and was provided immediate 
veterinary care. The 13 year old macaque was monitored in the hospital until her wounds healed and then discharged to 
her normal housing. Primary enclosures for nonhuman primates must be structurally sound for the species of nonhuman 
primates contained in them and kept in good repair. The cage weld that failed was no longer in good repair and allowed 
the animal to escape her enclosure and engage with the 13 year old female macaque that was injured.  
 
Corrected at the time of inspection on 4 March 2026 
3.80(b)(4)

Primary enclosures. 
In three nonhuman primate enclosures the animals were not housed in the space required by the regulation when housed 
together. A male and female macaque, both ~5 years old were housed together in a pair of enclosures that only provided 
4.3 square feet of space per animal. The male macaque weighed 10.3 kilograms (kg) according to the room log and 
required at least 6 feet of space, for a total of 10.3 square feet of space. In another pair of 4.3 square foot cages, an 11 
year old female was in one and a 9 year old female with a 7 month old infant (1.18kgs) was in the other enclosure. While 
the 8.6 square feet of space was sufficient for the adult females (both less than 10 kg), the 7 month old needed an 
additional 1.6 square feet of space per the regulations. In another pair of 4.3 square foot cages an ~8.5 year old female 
macaque (11.9 kgs) was housed with ~1.5 year old daughter (3.15 kgs), so total required square feet was 10.3 square 
feet and only 8.6 square feet was provided. Minimum space regulations are required by the Animal Welfare Act in order to 
ensure the welfare of the animals contained within. 
 
Corrected during inspection on 6 March 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the Attending Veterinarian and the IACUC staff. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Darren Rausch, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
